title: Freezing and extreme values: from RMT to number theory
speaker: Jon Keating

proof_stages.label: Triangular connection program
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames a three-way connection among random matrix theory, the Riemann zeta function, and statistical mechanics of random landscapes, with the aim of using freezing ideas to predict zeta extremes.

proof_stages.label: Local extreme-value problem for zeta
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the zeta function, the critical line, Selberg's central limit theorem for typical values, known upper and lower bounds for large values, and then defines the local maximum zeta_max(T) on intervals [T,T+2π].

proof_stages.label: CUE characteristic-polynomial analogue
proof_stages.summary: The CUE is introduced as the random-matrix model for zeta zeros and values, leading to the analogous random variable p_max(A), the maximum modulus of a CUE characteristic polynomial around the unit circle.

proof_stages.label: Random landscapes and freezing background
proof_stages.summary: The statistical-mechanics side is set up through random energy landscapes, Boltzmann partition functions, and freezing transitions, with emphasis on log-correlated Gaussian-type fields as the relevant difficult case.

proof_stages.label: Log-correlated partition-function identification
proof_stages.summary: Moments of characteristic polynomials and zeta over short intervals are rewritten as partition functions with energies given by minus log modulus, and trace expansions plus covariance computations show the same logarithmic correlations as the random-landscape model.

proof_stages.label: Moment calculation and freezing ansatz
proof_stages.summary: Using computable small-beta moments, Fisher-Hartwig/Selberg-integral asymptotics, and the assumption that the beta=1 singularity freezes, the speaker derives a conjectural law for log p_max with the correction log n - (3/4) log log n and a K0-Bessel fluctuation distribution.

proof_stages.label: Numerical tests for matrices and zeta
proof_stages.summary: The conjectural freezing predictions are compared with simulations of random matrices and high zeta-zero computations, testing the 3/4 logarithmic correction, the frozen free energy, and the K0-Bessel fluctuation law.

examples.name: Imaginary part of log zeta and zero-count fluctuations
examples.note: In response to a question about phase versus modulus, the speaker notes that Selberg's full complex-normal theorem also makes the imaginary part of log zeta describe fluctuations in the counting function of zeros, an aside not used later.
examples.engagement_reason: He only notes that the imaginary part of log zeta is Gaussian and encodes zero-count fluctuations, then says it will not be used.

passes.reason: The imaginary part of log zeta is used as an ordinary fluctuation observable for zero counts under Selberg’s CLT.
standard zero-count fluctuation statistic mentioned only as an aside
The imaginary part of log zeta is used as an ordinary zero-count fluctuation statistic, not as a failure case.
The imaginary part of log zeta is an ordinary fluctuation statistic for zero counts, mentioned as background rather than to refute a claim.
The imaginary part of log zeta is a standard observable encoding zero-count fluctuations, mentioned as background rather than to refute a claim.
The imaginary part of log zeta is used as a standard fluctuation statistic for zero counts, not as a structurally special object or a failure case.
The imaginary part of log zeta is cited as the companion Selberg-CLT statistic encoding zero-count fluctuations, not as a failure case.
Mentioned only as an aside to Selberg's theorem, and zero-count fluctuations are an ordinary component of log-zeta behavior.
used only as an aside about what the imaginary part of Selberg’s log-zeta theorem measures, not as a failure case or special structure
The imaginary part of log zeta is mentioned as an ordinary fluctuation observable encoding zero counts, not as a refutation or structurally special object.
Used only as an ancillary statistic of Selberg’s log-zeta theorem, with no structurally special feature singled out.

examples.name: Finite-height Riemann zeros matched to finite-size matrices
examples.note: After a scaling question, the speaker mentions numerical evidence that zeros at a fixed large height are modeled by finite-size CUE matrices when matrix size is matched to log of the height, illustrating the RMT-zeta correspondence.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes the finite-height/finite-size matching by equating mean densities and cites evidence, without carrying out a concrete finite-height comparison.

passes.reason: Finite-height Riemann zeros compared with finite-size random matrices are presented as a standard finite-parameter modeling correspondence, not as a special structural exception.
ordinary finite-height and finite-size comparison used as supporting evidence for the RMT-zeta link
Finite-height zeta zeros matched to finite-size random matrices are presented as supporting numerical evidence for the usual RMT analogy.
Finite-height zero data compared with finite-size CUE matrices is a representative finite-sample matching phenomenon used as supporting evidence.
Finite-height zeta zeros compared with finite-size random matrices are ordinary finite-parameter instances used as supporting evidence for the RMT analogy.
Finite-height Riemann zeros matched to finite-size matrices are presented as ordinary numerical evidence for the zeta–RMT analogy, not as an exceptional or adversarial case.
Finite-height zeros matched to finite-size matrices are used as numerical evidence for the zeta/RMT correspondence, with no intrinsic extremal or exceptional status.
Used as numerical/heuristic evidence for the zeta--random-matrix correspondence, with no intrinsic extreme or sporadic structure.
used as supporting evidence for the zeta–random-matrix analogy, with the finite-height/finite-size comparison not intrinsically extreme or sporadic
Finite-height zeros and finite-size matrices are used as supportive numerical evidence for the zeta/RMT correspondence, with no intrinsic extremal or exceptional structure singled out.
Finite-height zero data and finite-size CUE matrices are presented as supporting numerical evidence for the analogy, not as an obstruction or special object.

examples.name: CUE log-characteristic-polynomial central limit theorem
examples.note: To answer what it means for the two sides to match, the speaker gives the CLT for the normalized log modulus of the CUE characteristic polynomial as a concrete parallel to Selberg's theorem for zeta.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes the normalized CUE log-characteristic-polynomial expression, relates it to the zeta normalization, and later expands it via traces and computes its log-correlated covariance.

passes.reason: The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT is used as positive evidence for the zeta/RMT analogy, with CUE coming from the highly invariant Haar unitary ensemble.
Haar-unitary CUE setting is maximally unitary-invariant and used as positive evidence for the analogy
The CUE characteristic-polynomial CLT is a positive comparison theorem/evidence, and CUE is the Haar-unitary maximally invariant ensemble.
The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT concerns the Haar-unitary ensemble, a maximally invariant random-matrix model, and is used as positive evidence for the zeta analogy.
The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT comes from the Haar/unitarily invariant circular ensemble and is used as a positive model for zeta statistics.
The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT uses the highly invariant Haar unitary ensemble and serves as supporting evidence for matching Selberg’s theorem, not as a counterexample.
The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT is used as supporting evidence matching Selberg’s theorem, and the Haar-unitary CUE setting is highly invariant.
Used as positive evidence for matching zeta statistics, and the CUE/Haar unitary ensemble is distinguished by maximal unitary invariance.
used as positive evidence for matching zeta statistics, and the CUE side is the Haar-unitary invariant ensemble
The CUE log-characteristic-polynomial CLT is used as positive evidence for matching value distributions, and the CUE is a maximally invariant unitary ensemble.
The CUE setting is the Haar unitary ensemble with maximal unitary symmetry, and the CLT is used as positive evidence for the zeta–RMT match.
Used as corroborating evidence for matching zeta statistics, while the CUE setting is Haar/unitarily invariant and hence highly symmetric.

examples.name: Large-molecule configuration energy landscape
examples.note: The speaker invokes large molecules whose configurational energy depends sensitively on configuration to make the random-landscape statistical-mechanics problem physically concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: Large-molecule energy landscapes are invoked only as motivating physical examples of complex configuration energies.

passes.reason: A large-molecule energy landscape is a generic motivating example of a complex configuration space in statistical mechanics.
